mission.title: Install and Configure Calico on Kubernetes
mission.description: Calico is a CNI plugin and network policy engine that powers networking on a wide range of Kubernetes distributions including OpenShift, EKS, AKS, and GKE. Calico provides pod networking, network policy enforcement (both standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy and Calico's extended NetworkPolicy / GlobalNetworkPolicy CRDs), eBPF-based dataplane modes, and BGP peering for on-prem multi-cluster meshes. The recommended Kubernetes install path is the Tigera Operator chart, which manages an Installation Custom Resource that drives the Calico control plane and DaemonSet. This mission walks through installing the Operator, applying a baseline Installation CR, and the production hardening (encapsulation choice, MTU tuning, NetworkPolicy enforcement) that real clusters require.

steps.title: Add the Project Calico Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Project Calico Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add projectcalico https://docs.tigera.io/calico/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Tigera Operator
steps.description:
Install the Tigera Operator into a dedicated 'tigera-operator' namespace. The Operator owns the Installation CRD that drives Calico's control plane and DaemonSet.
```bash
helm install calico projectcalico/tigera-operator --version v3.32.0 \
  --namespace tigera-operator --create-namespace
```
Note: on existing Kubernetes clusters that already have a different CNI installed (kindnet on kind, kubenet, Cilium, Weave, Flannel, etc.), Calico will be installed alongside but will not become the active dataplane unless the existing CNI is removed and pods are restarted. To use Calico as the active CNI, install it on a fresh cluster created with no other CNI, or follow the upstream Calico migration guide for the existing-CNI replacement flow.

steps.title: Verify the Operator is running
steps.description:
Check that the Operator pod is running and the Installation CRD is registered.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace tigera-operator
kubectl get crd | grep operator.tigera.io
```

steps.title: Apply the Installation custom resource
steps.description:
The Operator reconciles a single 'Installation' CR named 'default' that defines the Calico topology. The Helm chart applies this CR automatically with sensible defaults, but you can re-apply your own to tune the encapsulation, IP pool CIDR, or NodeAddressAutodetectionV4 settings.
```bash
kubectl get installation default -o yaml
```
For a custom Installation, override on install or apply directly:
```yaml
apiVersion: operator.tigera.io/v1
kind: Installation
metadata:
  name: default
spec:
  calicoNetwork:
    ipPools:
    - cidr: <POD_CIDR>  # e.g. matches the kubelet --cluster-cidr
      encapsulation: VXLANCrossSubnet
      natOutgoing: Enabled
```

steps.title: Verify the Calico control-plane and DaemonSet pods
steps.description:
Once the Operator reconciles the Installation, it creates the calico-system namespace, deploys calico-kube-controllers, and rolls out a calico-node DaemonSet across the cluster. Wait for them to come up.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace calico-system
kubectl get installation default -o jsonpath='{.status.computed}'
```

steps.title: Apply a baseline NetworkPolicy to validate enforcement
steps.description:
Once Calico is the active CNI, NetworkPolicies are enforced by the calico-node dataplane. Apply a default-deny-all to a test namespace and confirm a Pod cannot reach the internet.
```bash
kubectl create namespace netpol-demo
kubectl run testpod --image=busybox --namespace netpol-demo --restart=Never --command -- sleep 600
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all
  namespace: netpol-demo
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
EOF
kubectl exec -n netpol-demo testpod -- wget -qO- --timeout=5 https://www.google.com
```
The wget should time out, which proves egress is blocked by the policy. Remove the policy and re-run to confirm traffic returns. (This step only meaningfully verifies enforcement when Calico is the active CNI — see the note in the install step about existing-CNI clusters.)

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Set resource limits on the Operator and on the calico-node DaemonSet via the Installation CR. The defaults are sized for medium-scale clusters. Tune up for large pod density or down for resource-constrained environments.
```bash
kubectl patch installation default --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"componentResources":[{"componentName":"Node","resourceRequirements":{"requests":{"cpu":"250m","memory":"100Mi"},"limits":{"cpu":"500m","memory":"500Mi"}}}]}}'
```

uninstall.title: Remove any custom NetworkPolicies
uninstall.description:
Drain custom Calico GlobalNetworkPolicy and tier resources before uninstalling the Operator so the control-plane can finalize them.
```bash
kubectl delete globalnetworkpolicies.crd.projectcalico.org --all
kubectl delete networksets.crd.projectcalico.org --all --all-namespaces
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Tigera Operator release. The Operator removes the Installation CR, which drains the calico-system namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall calico --namespace tigera-operator
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and namespaces
uninstall.description:
Removing the CRDs deletes any remaining tigera and projectcalico resources cluster-wide. Some chart versions auto-clean their CRDs on uninstall, in which case the delete prints NotFound and that is safe to ignore.
```bash
kubectl delete crd installations.operator.tigera.io
kubectl delete crd apiservers.operator.tigera.io
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd -o name | grep crd.projectcalico.org)
kubectl delete namespace tigera-operator calico-system calico-apiserver
```
Note: removing Calico from a cluster where it is the active CNI requires extra care. Pods will lose connectivity until a replacement CNI is installed. Either install a replacement CNI before uninstalling, or recreate the cluster.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Export the current Helm values and the Installation CR before upgrading.
```bash
helm get values calico --namespace tigera-operator > calico-backup.yaml
kubectl get installation default -o yaml > calico-installation-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the Project Calico Helm repository to pick up the latest chart.
```bash
helm repo update
helm search repo projectcalico/tigera-operator --versions | head -5
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Calico
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the release to the desired chart version. Always read the Calico release notes for breaking changes between minor versions, especially around dataplane behavior and CRD schema.
```bash
helm upgrade calico projectcalico/tigera-operator --namespace tigera-operator --version v3.32.0 --reuse-values
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Operator deployment rolled out, the Installation CR moved to the new computed state, and the calico-node DaemonSet rolled the new image cluster-wide.
```bash
kubectl rollout status deploy/tigera-operator -n tigera-operator
kubectl get installation default -o jsonpath='{.status.computed.calicoVersion}'
kubectl rollout status ds/calico-node -n calico-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Operator pod in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Operator pod will not start, check the logs for RBAC or webhook errors. The Operator needs cluster-admin equivalent permissions to install the Calico CRDs and reconcile the Installation.
```bash
kubectl logs -n tigera-operator deploy/tigera-operator --tail=200
```

troubleshooting.title: Installation stuck in Progressing state
troubleshooting.description:
If 'kubectl get installation default' shows Progressing for more than a few minutes, the Operator is unable to reconcile the Calico components. Inspect the conditions and the calico-system namespace.
```bash
kubectl get installation default -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
kubectl get pods -n calico-system
kubectl describe installation default | tail -30
```

troubleshooting.title: calico-node pods not starting on existing-CNI clusters
troubleshooting.description:
On clusters that already have a CNI (kindnet on kind, the cloud-provider's CNI on managed clusters), calico-node may fail with networking-related errors. Calico expects to be the active CNI. Either remove the existing CNI and restart pods, recreate the cluster with no CNI plugin so Calico installs cleanly, or use the upstream Calico migration guide.
```bash
kubectl logs -n calico-system ds/calico-node --tail=100
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kindnet
```

troubleshooting.title: NetworkPolicies appear to have no effect
troubleshooting.description:
If NetworkPolicies are applied but traffic is not blocked, Calico is likely not the active dataplane. Verify which CNI is enforcing policy.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n calico-system
kubectl describe node | grep -A2 'PodCIDR'
kubectl get installation default -o yaml | grep -A5 calicoNetwork
```

troubleshooting.title: Pod-to-pod connectivity broken after install
troubleshooting.description:
If pods cannot reach each other after Calico is installed alongside another CNI, the two dataplanes are conflicting. The fix is to fully remove one CNI before installing the other. Do not run two CNIs in parallel on the same cluster.
```bash
kubectl get pods -A -o wide | head -20
kubectl logs -n calico-system ds/calico-node --tail=100 | grep -i error
```

troubleshooting.title: High CPU usage on calico-node pods
troubleshooting.description:
calico-node CPU scales with the number of NetworkPolicies, IPSets, and BGP peers. Profile current usage and adjust resource limits via the Installation CR.
```bash
kubectl top pods -n calico-system
kubectl get installation default -o jsonpath='{.spec.componentResources}'
```

resolution.summary: A successful production install of Calico has the Tigera Operator pod Running in 'tigera-operator', the Installation CR in a Computed state with the configured calicoNetwork settings, the calico-system namespace populated with calico-kube-controllers and a calico-node DaemonSet on every node, calico-apiserver deployed, NetworkPolicies enforced by the calico-node dataplane, and the cluster pod CIDR matching the Installation's ipPools. From there platform teams can apply standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies, Calico GlobalNetworkPolicies, set up BGP peering for on-prem multi-cluster meshes, and switch to the eBPF dataplane mode where supported.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add projectcalico https://docs.tigera.io/calico/charts
helm repo update

helm install calico projectcalico/tigera-operator --version v3.32.0 \
  --namespace tigera-operator --create-namespace

prerequisites.description: A Kubernetes cluster with no existing CNI plugin (or with a clear migration plan from the existing CNI) is required for Calico to become the active dataplane. Helm and kubectl must be installed locally. For production, plan the encapsulation mode (VXLAN or IP-in-IP, with VXLANCrossSubnet as the typical default), the pod CIDR (must match what the kubelet was started with), and the BGP topology if you are running on-prem with route advertisement to upstream switches.
